title: Developer Productivity With and Without GitHub Copilot: A Longitudinal Mixed-Methods Case Study
study_design_description: A mixed-methods case study conducted at NAV IT, a large public sector agile organization, spanning a two-year period. The study combines quantitative GitHub activity data (26,317 commits from 703 repositories) with qualitative data including survey responses and interviews. Data was analyzed to compare developer activity before and after the introduction of GitHub Copilot.
primary_task_description: Analysis of 26,317 unique non-merge commits from 703 of NAV IT's GitHub repositories over a two-year period, focusing on commit-based activity metrics from 25 Copilot users and 14 non-users. Commit data was retrieved using custom Python scripts and the PyDriller framework, mining repositories for participants who provided their username in the survey.
population_description: The study was conducted at NAV IT, the technology division of a large Norwegian public sector organization with nearly one thousand employees. In September 2023, 100 software developers volunteered to receive a GitHub Copilot license, growing to 250 by May 2025. A total of 63 participants provided their username for the study. The cleaned dataset consisted of 39 developers: 25 Copilot users and 14 non-users. Thirteen interviews were conducted in November and December 2023, each lasting between 30 and 60 minutes with an average duration of 47 minutes. A survey was distributed between March and April 2024.

arms.description: Developers who had not adopted GitHub Copilot

outcomes.outcome_description: Survey question asking Copilot adopters: "Since getting access to GitHub Copilot, have you noticed a change in the following: Your own productivity" measured on a scale from "Major decrease" (-2) to "Major increase" (+2)
Number of commits per week, aggregated into weekly chunks with weeks containing no commits manually imputed as "0"
Number of lines of code added per week, aggregated into weekly chunks
Number of lines of code deleted per week, aggregated into weekly chunks
Net change in lines of code (insertions minus deletions) per week, measured as average weekly net diff after adoption minus average weekly net diff before adoption
Overall developer activity comparing Copilot users and non-users before and after adoption

reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: The Mann–Whitney U tests comparing Copilot users and non-users also confirmed that Copilot users were significantly more active than non-users (p <0.00555), both before and after adoption.

Before Copilot's launch, users were adding 188 lines and deleting 105 lines per week on average, compared to non-users' 80 additions and 40 deletions. After Copilot's launch, the user group's insertions ticked up slightly (to 200/week) while deletions ticked down (98/week), yielding an increase in net lines of code (+16 net lines as average weekly).

After Copilot's launch, the user group's insertions ticked up slightly (to 200/week) while deletions ticked down (98/week), yielding an increase in net lines of code (+16 net lines as average weekly).

After Copilot's launch, the user group's insertions ticked up slightly (to 200/week) while deletions ticked down (98/week), yielding an increase in net lines of code (+16 net lines as average weekly).
Non-users, by contrast, showed a small decline in both additions (70) and deletions (30) in the same period (thus a roughly flat net change).

Although a slight upward trend is visible, the correlation is not statistically significant (Spearman ρ≈0.17, p= 0.40).

The strongest correlation was observed for commit count, with a ρ= 0.17, which is considered a weak correlation, but with a p-value of 0.406, which did not allow us to reject the null hypothesis when using a Bonferroni corrected threshold for six tests of α= 0.0083.

Interestingly, the one activity metric that showed a negligible correlation with perceived productivity was net lines of code (ΔDiff, ρ≈0.09, where ΔDiff is average weekly net diff after adoption minus average weekly net diff before adoption).

notes: This is a longitudinal mixed-methods case study with a self-selection bias: Copilot users (n=25) were already significantly more active than non-users (n=14) before Copilot adoption (Mann–Whitney U, p < 0.00555). The final dataset consisted of N=4095 weekly observations based on 105 weeks of activity data for each of the 39 developers. The paper found no statistically significant correlations between objective commit-based metrics and self-reported productivity changes across all six activity metrics examined (Bonferroni corrected α = 0.0083). The dataset was cleaned by removing duplicates, trimming outliers at the 95th percentile, and excluding users with fewer than one commit per week on average. The paper notes that code quality metrics (structural metrics such as function complexity and average module size) showed no significant differences between users and non-users. The discrepancy between objective metrics and subjective experience is highlighted as a key finding, with the authors noting that developer work patterns vary significantly and that many responsibilities (especially for those in managerial roles) are less focused on writing code.
